message.abstract:
<jats:title>Abstract</jats:title>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Introduction</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>Coronavirus (COVID-19) has dramatically changed the landscape of dentistry including Paediatric Dentistry. This paper explores paediatric patient data within a wider service evaluation completed within an Urgent Dental Care Centre in the North East of England and North Cumbria over a 6-week period.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Aim</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>To assess demand for the service, patient demographics and inform paediatric urgent dental care pathways.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Main outcome methods</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>
                    Data collected included key characteristics of paediatric patients accessing Paediatric Dental Services from 23
                    <jats:sup>rd</jats:sup>
                    March to 3
                    <jats:sup>rd</jats:sup>
                    May 2020. Descriptive statistics were used for analysis.
                  </jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Results</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>There were 369 consultations (207 telephone, 124 face-to-face and 38 Out of Hours consultations). The mean age of children accessing the service was 7 years old. 7% of those attending face-to-face visits were reattenders. The most common diagnoses were irreversible pulpitis and dental trauma. 49% of face-to-face consultations resulted in extractions, 28% with General Anaesthetic, and 21% with Local Anaesthetic.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Conclusion</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>Management of dental emergencies provided by the Urgent Dental Care Centre for paediatric patients has largely been effective and confirmed the efficacy of patient pathways established.</jats:p>
                  <jats:list list-type="simple">
                    <jats:title>Three in Brief Points</jats:title>
                    <jats:list-item>
                      <jats:p>⍰ Describes the approach adopted in the North East of England and North Cumbria to managing paediatric dental emergencies during the coronavirus pandemic</jats:p>
                    </jats:list-item>
                    <jats:list-item>
                      <jats:p>⍰ Provides an overview of dental problems and management provided to paediatric patients in the first 6 weeks of the coronavirus pandemic</jats:p>
                    </jats:list-item>
                    <jats:list-item>
                      <jats:p>⍰ Confirms the need for general anaesthetic services for exodontia in the paediatric population</jats:p>
                    </jats:list-item>
                  </jats:list>
                </jats:sec>
title: Paediatric Dentistry and the coronavirus (COVID-19) response in the North East of England and North Cumbria
